But for individuals it doesn't matter. It's an open-source project anyway — so what if my code is terrible? Come help me write it.

2026 的方向：finite training, infinite usage

What are your thoughts on 2026 and what are your expectations?

I sometimes really love saying this slogan: models should achieve "train with finite context, use as infinite context" (有限训练，无限使用). In other words, you use this limited context length to train it, but in usage, it can use a very, very long, even nearly infinite context length.

I think this has a chance of being realized this year. Once this is achieved, it will unlock many new applications. The simplest example: you could potentially let this model interact with you continuously and continuously receive your information. As it runs, it will continuously evaluate the current context and your conversation and possibly discard information it deems unimportant. Then it becomes the personal assistant everyone dreams of.

Technically speaking, I think this will definitely be realized this year no matter what. What people haven't reached consensus on yet is how to technically achieve this. There are many technical paths, but right now it's more about trying to see which path can work. There might be several paths that all work — we'll have to test them experimentally under common user scenarios to see which path is the most efficient.

模型进步是否在放缓？

Standing here in Q1 2026, as a frontline researcher, do you think the pace of model improvement is slowing down?

I think not at all.

It's hard to say quantitatively because you need to give me a standard. If the standard is "I just look at some Benchmark like SWE-bench — how many points it gains each month" — then this will definitely slow down, because by definition this Benchmark maxes out at 100%. The closer you get, the slower it definitely gets. But this doesn't necessarily mean users feel the model's capability growth has slowed.

Going from 50% to 60% — it might feel like, hey, that's a bit better. But quite possibly, from 70% to 75%, the gains feel even greater than from 50% to 60%. If it's from 80% to 90%, or 90% to 100%, the difference would feel even more significant.

I think it's based on my personal feeling as a researcher. My impression is the model's ability to learn things is getting stronger and stronger. It used to take a lot of effort to get the model to learn to do something. But now it probably doesn't require that much effort. The most important thing is you need to clearly define the problem and figure out how to build the right data.

Pre-training 没到天花板

Why is the learning ability getting stronger?

One reason is pre-training. Actually, over the past few months I think it has been getting stronger. This might be somewhat controversial in a sense, because a few months ago many people were already discussing whether this Scaling Law had reached its limit. My experience is that it hasn't. And my feeling is in the next four months, I don't see any signs of it ending either.

When someone thinks a pattern has reached its limit, it's basically one of these situations:

  1. They feel the applicable range of this pattern has reached its limit — Scaling Law simply can't extend infinitely.
  2. They feel that one of the conditions can no longer be met — for example, data has already hit a wall.
  3. There's a bug somewhere in their work that they haven't noticed themselves, so they think it's reached its limit.

From my observation, I think the vast majority of people who hit a wall — it's because of the third reason. It's because there's a bug.

Bug 的本质

What kind of bug?

One possibility: when you're working on Scaling Laws, some scientific assumptions weren't quite right. For example, what kind of token horizon you choose — that is, for each model size, what kind of expected training data volume you pick. Where this data comes from. It's possible that these more scientific choices weren't made clearly.

Another possibility: there's simply a bug. Actually, I don't think this is surprising in the industry. Many times, fixing a single bug — the progress it brings is far greater than some fancy tricks.

How do you deal with bugs? I feel like this is more of a mindset issue. When you encounter a bug, if you think it can't be fixed, you'll say we've hit a wall. When you encounter a bug, if you think "oh, this can definitely be fixed," then you'll feel like we haven't hit a wall yet.

A more important thing is the working system. That is, when something is different from what you predicted, can you systematically rule out various possibilities? I think this is a very important thing. This is something Gemini and Anthropic do well. Especially in pre-training, when behavior at a certain scale might be different from what you imagined, people can design reasonable ablation experiments (消融实验) to test whether some of your imagined possible factors are actually the real factors.

模型能力的驱动力：数据、算力、算法

You think model capabilities can still improve. Then its driving force — data and compute, algorithms — which do you think is the main driving force?

I think they all contribute. But in a sense, data and compute are two things that are actually very strongly correlated. When your compute goes up, you'll naturally attract more data. When data goes up, you'll naturally need more compute.

For algorithms, algorithmic progress often has a phase transition. There's a phase where you haven't figured out what to do at all — at that stage, algorithms are extremely critical. At a certain point you might discover the most important thing in the algorithm. Then it might suddenly go from completely impossible to possible. After that, algorithmic improvements are more of a gradual improvement — improving computational efficiency or the efficiency of using data.

From the perspective of language model pre-training, the leap was the development of the Transformer. After the Transformer was discovered, it's been mostly gradual and smooth improvement.

Within the relatively clear frameworks we have now — pre-training, post-training (whether based on reinforcement learning or supervised learning) — compute and data are the main drivers.

But in some other directions, the driving factors might be different. For example, multimodal generation — algorithmically speaking, hasn't been fully figured out yet. So that's still a scientific problem that hasn't been solved.

But language is no longer a scientific problem. Natural language generation, before this technical approach hits a wall, is relatively clear scientifically. But in terms of engineering, there's still so, so, so much to be done.

Pre-training 还能走多远？

How much more do you think pre-training can improve?

When you haven't hit the wall, you don't actually know how long the road is. What I can see is that we haven't hit the wall yet. But I don't know when we'll hit it either.

If I really had to estimate a timeline — I think the next four months will still see progress. But in the AI field, no one can predict what happens after four months.

For people working on models, we get more excited about this kind of model progress. People at Anthropic and Google, or Gemini, are probably thinking more about how our AI will keep progressing, and soon we'll be replaced — and after being replaced, what should we do? — rather than worrying about what to do when models hit a wall.

为何 Coding 进展最快？

Why is coding developing the fastest?

Coding itself hasn't just been developing the fastest over the past few months. From Claude 3.5 (new), or what some called Claude 3.6, around October of the year before last, it's been in a state of rapid development ever since.

The coding scenario has two biggest advantages:

First advantage: the reward signal is very well-defined. For example, if a software engineer task requires writing code to implement a feature, that feature needs certain inputs and produces certain outputs. This is very easy to test. Your input and output match up — it means your implementation is successful. If not, then it's unsuccessful.

Second advantage: coding data has a very natural foundation — GitHub. GitHub has aggregated over the past few decades a lot of high-quality code written by many excellent programmers. Starting from that code, you can build a tremendous number of environments.

From a product perspective, there's another reason: coding demand is in a sense relatively singular. It's not like building social media or games where everyone might have different tastes. The good thing is that excellent programmers writing code actually have fairly similar styles — clean and concise, structurally clear, suitable for future development, with reasonable abstractions. Good programmers tend to have a fairly consensus-driven standard. So from a product perspective, it makes the coding product much simpler.

个人编码工作流：90%+ 来自 AI

In your current work, what percentage of code do you write with Claude Code? How many times more productive does it make you?

You just asked a question that almost got me fired — Google doesn't allow using Claude Code.

For me, a conservative estimate — maybe 90% of the code is model-generated. But I need to spend a lot of time reviewing the code, to see if it's written appropriately, written reasonably, whether it's really what I wanted it to write.

After having AI-assisted tools, the most important thing about writing code has become how you design it — how you design the logic of your code and which files it needs to interact with, what things need to be done, and you need to give the model some reasonable context.

Actually outputting code — I think models are way more capable than humans. If you count: how many lines of code I wrote by hand vs. the model wrote — I'd say conservatively, the model wrote over 90%. If not conservative, maybe 99% or 100%.

A year and a half ago, only Claude was able to actually write this kind of software engineering code. At that time, you could still feel many flaws in the model. For example, sometimes when it wrote code, it would only focus on this one file — it wouldn't pay much attention to relationships between multiple files. If a class definition was buried many layers deep, the model probably couldn't find it. Now this is happening less and less.

效率提升与工作时长悖论

From the perspective of writing code, it's quite hard to quantify. But if we talk about running experiments and the efficiency of implementing ideas, compared to a year or even a year and a half ago, it could be 20 or even 50 times faster.

Because models have really become — you can open several at the same time, you have several ideas and test them simultaneously. Sometimes the model can help you monitor some experiments. It's quite a significant efficiency boost.

But if we talk about personal working hours, I feel like it has made my working hours longer. Because development speed has increased — the more you try, the more you want to try. There are more and more ideas to try.

Before, you might have had this situation: you have a file you haven't seen before, you might not quite understand it yourself, you'd definitely have to spend time finding that person and schedule, maybe a few hours later. But now it's not like that. You just see this file, you don't understand it, just ask Claude or Gemini — Gemini might tell you the result in five seconds. So in terms of working hours, working hours have actually gotten longer, and the intensity has increased too.

Well, Google isn't that Google anymore. Not that work-life balance Google. In the GenAI field, no one can just coast along.

个人作息

I usually start around 9 in the morning, get to the office at 9. At 9 AM, I might first get up and check emails and look at the experiments from the night before. Then get to the office around 10. At night, if I'm alone in the US, I might stay until around 10 or 11. If my family is here, if my wife is here, I might go home a bit earlier. But at home, I'd be working anyway.

In the GenAI field, no one is just lying around. Unless you've completely lost interest in technology and have no ambition for yourself. Then no one would care if you just lay there. But I think most people are quite self-driven.

下一个 Coding 时刻在哪？

Where will the next explosion happen after coding?

You asked a good question. If I could see it clearly, I might have gone out to start a company already.

But it's true that besides coding, we can already see that many other directions are already having a big impact. If we only talk about those directions, they might not be a good market direction. Coding is special in that it itself is a very large market.

For example, some people say the next direction is AI-generated content. But how big is that market? If this content is for people to consume, then people have limited time. No matter how much content you generate, people's time is only 24 hours a day. Unless it completely replaces people — like replacing TV — then that would be another story.

I think the most likely one might be this kind of interactive education.

编码：手段还是目的？

There are two types of people. One type — they genuinely want to make coding better. Another type — they want to use coding as a means to validate AI capabilities. Both directions are fine. But people who genuinely want to make coding better need to think more about products. People who want to validate AI capabilities need to think more about how to build better benchmarks.

为什么 PM 难以被 AI 替代？

To be honest, I think being a good product manager is something I currently can't figure out how to train AI to do.

There's no standard. Like what makes a good product — I can't really figure it out. There's no very objective standard. You have to build it and let people use it. Only then do you know it's good. That's something with very unclear feedback signals.

程序员会被完全取代吗？

I think that day will come. But it won't come all at once. It won't be like programmers are all still there and after one night, the next day all programmers are fired. It will definitely be a gradual process.

Some companies have already started laying people off. In a sense, AI is a very centralized technology. It will make a small number of people stronger but will make most people lose their unique value. For traditional software engineering, the final result might be: now 1/1000 of the people do the work of everyone in the past, earning 100 times the current salary.

Advice for programmers: Embrace new things. Very important. One very important thing for future programmers might be how to effectively collaborate with AI.

Future good programmers — first, technically speaking, they will definitely be very strong. Because if you're technically weak, there's no reason why AI can't replace you. But being technically strong might not be the only thing — it might be a sufficient condition, not necessary. Another important thing: you have to understand how your part of the work fits into a large organization or a big company. Their planning ability — if strong, they can take a complex thing and break it down into many smaller things and hand them over to different AIs.

Right now these three abilities seem important. But it doesn't mean AI won't be able to in six months.

Seedance 与字节多模态生成

Will Seedance make Google anxious?

Possibly yes. But this anxiety hasn't reached me yet. Maybe it gives the Google DeepMind team responsible for multimodal generation some pressure.

I might not think they have much to be anxious about. It doesn't reflect any paradigm shift. ByteDance — whether it's product effect or in terms of data, these details are done very, very well. ByteDance has historically had a relatively strong advantage in multimodal generation. But at least personally, I haven't experienced that it's a paradigm shift.

Does Seedance's product capability come from model capability or product capability?

If you ask me to guess, the model probably accounts for the majority. Good model capability comes from data. There probably isn't fundamental innovation in algorithms — multimodal still belongs to that scientific problem. Compared to text tokens, the paradigm isn't that fixed yet. In generation, maybe each company uses somewhat different techniques. Right now we can mostly see in terms of effects, ByteDance and Google DeepMind are the two that do it better.

评价 Wu Yonghui（ByteDance Seed lead）

After I joined Gemini, I saw more of Yonghui's good side. By looking at his past code commits and the projects he's led, my feeling is that he's one of the few people I've met — at such a high level, also very senior, yet still has very strong technical skills. That's extremely rare. I'm probably not yet at the level to evaluate Yonghui at that level. But if you ask me — Yonghui is extremely strong.

中美模型差距

If we take a snapshot right now and look at the development trends over the past year or year and a half, obviously the gap between China and the US is getting smaller and smaller. But whether this gap will eventually close completely or even if China surpasses the US — I think that's an open question.

China is indeed at a significant disadvantage in terms of actual compute resources. But this disadvantage may have actually forced out some interesting things. Chinese model companies are quite good at distilling from others.

蒸馏的两种方式：暴力 vs 聪明

Recently Dario (Anthropic Co-founder and CEO) called out three companies for distilling from them. Distillation itself is actually an open secret. But there are different ways to approach distillation — brute-force distillation and smart distillation.

Brute-force distillation: taking a bunch of tokens generated by Claude and forcibly training on them. It's not very ethical from a business standpoint, and intellectually rather foolish — companies doing this essentially demonstrate they don't even know what they want to do. The only thing they can do is copy others and make their model look a bit better on the benchmarks.

But distillation also involves some very interesting scientific questions. For example: in my process of generating my own training data pipeline, I use other models as assistants. Or the answers generated by my own model use other models as evaluators. This is commercially a bit of a gray area, but technically quite interesting.

Chinese labs may have become pioneers in Multi-Agent training. If they use models from different companies with smarter approaches and integrate them into a single training system, each model's distribution might be very different. This is true Multi-Agent. It might be more so than, for example, using several Geminis together.

哪家公司用什么样的蒸馏？

[Names bleeped]

First of all, I haven't worked in a Chinese lab. But my feeling is XXX probably used hard distillation. And XXX might have done hard distillation before but later probably gradually tried to shift toward soft distillation. The one that probably distills less is ByteDance. ByteDance's model is still quite distinctive.

豆包：声音生成与日常对话

Doubao is definitely not as smart as Gemini or Claude. But Doubao's voice generation is extremely good — technically Doubao is indeed the best at it.

Wait, is that difficult? In the US, people are more focused on how to improve work efficiency. The questions I have more often in life are all technical ones — asking a smart model like Gemini is the best.

But like when you're cooking — you might run into some problem. You might need someone to tell you right away but you don't have such a person. Those — I think it's probably more of a data issue.

I think the voice quality might be — to put it politely, probably one of the best in the world. To put it bluntly, simply the best in the world.

Another aspect: it generates very fast, which is very important. Many models are showing you their chain of thought. But I'm talking about trivial things in your daily life — I don't want to see its chain of thought. If you try Gemini 2.5 Pro and Gemini 2.5 Flash, you'll find Gemini 2.5 Flash, when completing the same problem, is much faster than before and with less fluff. It's more about when to pay attention to it and do something about it.

Maybe now these American companies are all still in the stage of working hard to push the upper limits of intelligence forward. And ByteDance — of course it's also pushing the upper limits, but it might just be doing very well in user optimization too.

豆包手机

I think it's a great idea. Personally, in terms of results, they actually did a pretty good job.

What I don't know is technically, how well optimized it is. From a results perspective, there's no problem. But I don't know how much overhead it has. If that overhead is very, very large, then it's probably a technical issue that needs to be solved. Because you don't want your model to book a high-speed train ticket for you and end up costing more than the ticket itself.

Apple probably wanted to do something like this before. It's just that Apple's own models haven't been that great. Now they might be trying to do this through a partnership with Gemini.

中国机器人

I've watched some performances, also searched for some prices on Amazon. I was really surprised they're so cheap.

I used to think — at the software level there's nothing really, but mainly hardware. I thought for hardware to be this mature, it would probably cost something like several million dollars. But when I checked, the price is much cheaper than that.

This reflects China's hardware industry chain still has a lot of advantages.

In terms of hardware I think it's indeed very very strong. From the software perspective — I think robot models are probably more in the feature engineering era. You have a given environment, a given scenario, you optimize for that scenario. Doing RL, building appropriate virtual environments — you can improve, but it doesn't have strong generalization.

Whether there is generalization is actually a watershed for many AI directions. Language models, after Transformer and GPT, entered that kind of stage — crossed a threshold where you can improve all abilities by improving at one level. I think robots haven't reached that stage. They're more before that stage.

I highly recommend everyone go check out robotics labs — they're way more interesting than language model labs. Language model labs feel like normal offices. But robotics labs really have people controlling these robots, collecting all kinds of data, and watching the robot picking up items off shelves.

In terms of LLM years, robotics hasn't reached the GPT-1 moment yet. Definitely not. Whether it's robotics or multimodal generation, neither has reached that point.

童年与少年：从宁夏到上海

Where did you grow up?

I was born in Ningxia, in a very, very small city called Dawukou. See, that confused expression of yours already shows how small this city is. This city existed in the past because of a coal mine — also because of Shitanjing, a coal mine. So I was born there.

I went to Shanghai with my parents during elementary school. The latter half of elementary school and my middle and high school were in Shanghai. Then I went to Beijing for undergrad.

I didn't get into university through physics competition. I think I was quite mediocre when I was young.

The middle school I attended was called Shangnan Middle School East Campus — another school that leaves people baffled. In a small environment within one class, there were still some classmates who wanted to do things properly. But overall I think that middle school was in a relatively laid-back state.

高中：放着四校不去，选竞赛班

My grades could get me into the four top schools, but couldn't get into the best classes in these four schools. But at that time I really wanted to do competitions, because I had never done competitions before.

Why did you want to do competitions if you never did them?

Because I never did them, so I wanted to do them. My personality is — I always love doing things I'm not good at.

I felt that before going to college, I should give it a try. But my grades weren't good enough for that. Then I discovered there was a slightly worse school — Gezhi High School. That school had a competition class. I felt this competition class — in today's terms it's an underdog. In the words of that time, I felt like the barefoot aren't afraid of those wearing shoes.

So actually at that time, Shanghai still had this early admission system, where before the high school entrance exam you could sign a contract with a school and reserve a spot in advance.

进清华：一条短信改变命运

At that time the competition direct admission system had already declined significantly. Only those who made the national training team could get direct admission. I wasn't at the level of making the national training team.

But before taking the senior year competition exam, by a twist of fate I went to Tsinghua for a summer camp. On the last day of the summer camp, I heard they were doing independent enrollment, but mainly aimed at Beijing students.

I frantically texted the admissions office teacher saying I wanted to take the exam with them. He agreed — agreed to let us take the exam.

What reason did you use to convince him?

I've forgotten the specifics, but the general idea of that text was: "You give Beijing students the exam — why not give Shanghai students the exam?"

I didn't think they were playing favorites. I just felt they had this opportunity — why not give it to us? Everyone's competing on the same playing field.

So I sent this message and they actually let us take the exam. From our high school I was the one who sent it.

The most important life lesson I learned: be bold. If you don't fight for it, you'll never get it. Even if you fight for it, you might not get it. But if you don't fight for it, you definitely won't get it.

The day I heard about it, I immediately started frantically texting. Why do I always have quite a soft spot for Tsinghua? I just feel that this school is willing to give people opportunities — to provide equal opportunities for everyone.

When I came out, I felt I totally bombed it. Because I couldn't solve half a problem. But later I found out others missed even more. So I did get in after all.

Independent recruitment lowered the cutoff to the first-tier university line. Sure enough, my gaokao wasn't high enough for Tsinghua, but I could get into any school except Tsinghua and Peking University.

父母的态度

My parents — the best thing about my parents is they didn't really interfere much. They may have tried to control me at some point but later found they couldn't. I just didn't listen to them.

Most Chinese families — it's already considered pretty good when kids discuss things with their parents. I usually just informed them. Including filling out applications for high school and college — my parents might not have even seen my application forms.

I think they just — when you can't understand what someone is doing, the best thing is to not meddle. I think my parents understood this very well.

性格：竞争 + 自虐

I think I am pretty rebellious. My personality — I really care about what I want to do. If it's something I've figured out I want to do, don't try to stop me, and I'll definitely do my absolute best. But if it's something I don't want to do, forcing me won't help, I won't do it.

Pretty strong (competitive). But I think I'm more competing with myself, pushing myself. Not really willing to compete with others. Of course, if it's something I think is important and you also think is important, then I definitely have to outdo you.

物理之路：清华学堂班 → 凝聚态理论

I was doing condensed matter theory at the time. At that time we were in the Jixian class. The Jixian class had a very good tradition: although it was in the physics department, it didn't restrict what students could do. Actually 2/3 of the students wouldn't do physics.

Another good tradition: it encouraged students to enter research labs as early as possible and learn through research.

I really wanted to do theory. The teacher recommended: "Hey, the Institute for Advanced Study is a great place." Tsinghua Institute for Advanced Study, the research institute founded by Mr. Chen-Ning Yang.

There happened to be a teacher who was still young at that time called Wang Zhong — he was my undergraduate teacher. At that time he didn't have many students either. He was quite patient and gave me some papers to read.

Later I discovered condensed matter theory — especially the project we were doing was related to topological insulators. It was a direction very suitable for undergraduates to get started with. You only needed to know the most basic things: quantum mechanics, statistical mechanics, solid state physics. But it might really test the depth of your understanding.

The work we did together — possibly the work in open quantum systems — looking back now is still quite important work.

Doing that work, doing research during that period, is actually very, very similar to doing AI now. You have an idea, you have an understanding, and at that stage you can do a numerical experiment to verify whether your idea and understanding are correct. AI is the same — you have an idea, you have an understanding, you design some experiments to verify whether your understanding is correct, and then you design some model training pipeline to implement your ideas.

非厄米系统：一个范式转移

(Try to speak in human terms — but it's also possible I'll be talking nonsense. Those who don't want to listen can skip ahead.)

One of the most basic assumptions of quantum mechanics is: an isolated system, its evolution is described by unitary evolution. This linear process can be described by an operator called the Hamiltonian. The Hamiltonian, in a certain sense, is somewhat like the energy of the system, but not exactly.

If it's an isolated system, this Hamiltonian will be a Hermitian matrix — one where you transpose it and take the complex conjugate, and it's the same as the original.

But real systems — the vast majority are not isolated systems. For example, you, me, as human beings — definitely have to exchange information and matter with the outside world. Materials are the same. If you put a piece of material there, unless you create an extremely high vacuum, you always have to interact with the substrate.

So real systems — corresponding Hamiltonian won't be Hermitian either. That's where the term "non-Hermitian" comes from. It's essentially for studying open quantum systems — quantum systems that exchange with the outside world.

We were initially trying to study some topological phenomena in these open quantum systems. We found the theoretical results from hand calculations just couldn't match the numerical results no matter what. The hand calculation assumed periodic boundary conditions (on a ring or torus surface). Numerically, it would calculate with open boundaries (closer to actual situations like a square material). These two results just couldn't be reconciled.

Later we found: the basic paradigm people used to describe Hermitian systems — the so-called Bloch wave assumption — that eigenstates are linear combinations of sine and cosine waves — in non-Hermitian systems, breaks down. The fact is, in non-Hermitian systems, energy eigenstates can all potentially accumulate at one edge of the system.

We systematically established this set of descriptive methods and built a framework to describe a non-Hermitian system with open boundaries.

为什么不继续做下去？

Why didn't you continue with it? It's hard to catch a paradigm shift, isn't it?

It's the weakness of human nature. I always love challenging myself with things I don't know. Maybe looking back at that work a few years later, it would become the most important work in that direction. Later when you do some more work, it might indeed make you more famous, get more citations, write more good journal articles, find a good faculty position. But for a scientific career, it wouldn't be that exciting.

So at that time I wanted to switch to something else — switch to something I wasn't good at, do it right. So when doing my PhD, I switched directions to do high energy theory.

PhD 大教训：远离没有客观评判的领域

To put it badly, I love torturing myself. To put it nicely, challenging myself.

I think the longer I stayed in that field, the more I felt this thing was stupid. A person's life isn't that long. Why waste your own time serving old-timers?

I learned a lot, grew a lot. But for this world, it didn't produce any contribution. High energy theory direction — very, very difficult. The bad thing about it: it's actually not particularly verifiable. There are no objective evaluation criteria. Experiments completely can't catch up to what you're discussing in theory — whether energy scales or microscopic scales.

How does it progress? One source comes from mathematical self-consistency. For example, you study string theory. Naturally everyone asks: can string theory at low energy return to quantum field theory and then to classical physics?

But when this field completely lacks experiments and objective standards, there definitely won't be just one self-consistent framework. Who does well, who doesn't — actually depends on the subjective judgments of some old-timers in the field.

So 5 years' PhD bought a big lesson: do things with relatively objective evaluation criteria — or from another perspective, do things that can have an impact on this world.

暗中保持外部满分

In terms of actual research output — no one would say my PhD papers were bad. But if I'm being completely honest, how much impact did they have on the world? Almost none.

For me personally, I was really unhappy with that. But I also wasn't unhappy enough to worry that people would say I was slacking off. I really wasn't slacking off.

Meeting external expectations, or meeting the standards of a small circle — it's like training a model. Once you're in that small circle and you know what their evaluation criteria are, it's easy to do well, even if you don't actually believe in those standards. But deep down, you know you don't buy into them.

I eventually realized I couldn't fool myself. I started having that feeling probably around the last two years of my PhD. But back then I hadn't figured out what to do if not this.

在量子计算与 AI 之间选择 AI

I spent some time exploring different directions. At first I mostly looked into quantum computing or quantum information. Then I got a postdoc offer. After getting the postdoc offer, it felt more urgent. Because when you're still in school, you can still have a student mindset. After leaving school, it's your own career.

I felt quantum computing and AI were two directions that offer young people more opportunities.

My postdoc had no direction — basically just theoretical physics. A postdoc is a very independent position. You basically do whatever you want. In a way, it's kind of like doing charity — government or private organizations donate money to the university, school uses it to hire postdocs.

I was at Berkeley for two or three months in reality. But officially, I was only there for two weeks. I had actually already gone there before I officially started. But after I officially started I only stayed for two weeks before quitting.

The people at Berkeley were too nice. They said: "No worries, just wait until things are settled. Come for as long as you can." I told them I might go do AI.

物理对做 AI 的帮助

In terms of hard skills there wasn't much help. In terms of pure tool-based skills, actually the transfer from physics to AI is very, very little.

If you really have to ask — I think maybe personality. Physics people want to get to the bottom of things more, want to understand something more, and want to do things more systematically. Because we're used to this very systematic experimental or theoretical methods.

But I don't think this is unique to physics. Many computer science, chemistry, biology people also have this trait.

AI 与黑箱：科学视角下的理解

Everything in this world is a black box. Even physics — something everyone thinks they understand actually doesn't really have an understanding from microscopic behavior to macroscopic manifestations.

AI is the same. Whether it's a black box or not is actually all relative. We indeed don't understand language models to the level of neurosurgery-level precision — saying this behavior is caused by which neuron, which artificial neuron's which activation. Except in some very sparse, very small networks (like Anthropic's interpretability team), we haven't reached such understanding.

But it doesn't mean we have no understanding at all. For example, Scaling Law describes how models at that scale, with model size and data, improve in perplexity. If Scaling Law doesn't count as a small part of understanding, then can we also say we don't understand this world at all?

Scaling Law is an empirical law. But the boundary between empirical laws and scientific laws is quite blurry. Look back at thermodynamic laws — the first law, second law, Clapeyron equation — when first discovered, they were also empirical laws. Later as time went by, we gradually understood their microscopic mechanisms.

智能涌现：一个不科学的术语

Can you explain in scientific terms this so-called intelligence emergence?

First of all, this term itself isn't very scientific. So naturally there's no way to use scientific language to describe something unscientific.

When many people talk about intelligence emergence, what they have in mind: previous language models could only do one type of thing — only translation, only analysis. But now it seems like the model can do everything.

To me, it's more of a technical emergence rather than a behavioral emergence. Through research we discovered how to do this kind of large-scale training and lift all capabilities across the board.

The only qualitative difference is whether there's been a technical breakthrough that allows us to scale up and lift all capabilities across the board.

为什么选 AI 而不是量子计算？

Quantum computing's main bottleneck right now is actually in the experiments. It's not about how you design algorithms — it's more about how you implement it experimentally. That's something I'm actually not good at.

AI, as I just mentioned, is more about having an idea and then you can use some numbers to verify it. This is actually quite similar to doing physics. It even is.

That's why I've always liked to compare this with 18th century physics. In that era, theory and experiment weren't separated. There were no theoretical physicists, experimental physicists. You just did physics. You could do experiments yourself and also do theoretical speculation. I think AI is a bit like that era.

So actually, the distance from theoretical physics to experimental physics is farther than directly jumping to AI.

加入 Anthropic：物理背景的渠道

I had former colleagues at Anthropic. Anthropic actually has a lot of people from physics backgrounds, especially theoretical physics backgrounds.

Why? From my personal perspective, the main reason is still connections. In Anthropic's founding team there were actually three or four fairly technical people at the time, and two of them are still very much on the technical front lines in leadership. Both came from physics backgrounds. The people they recruited also came from physics backgrounds.

But after I joined, they barely hired anymore people with no AI background at all. So it's a product of its era.

面试 Anthropic：白手起步 nanoGPT

So I tried to reach out to a few places. I reached out to OpenAI and Google DeepMind. But Google DeepMind, because it was too slow back then, just didn't end up in consideration.

Anthropic — my first manager used to do theoretical physics too. He said: "We're trying to do reinforcement learning, large-scale reinforcement learning. There are many scientific questions to understand."

That was August or September '24. Back then most people didn't really know how to do it. Because o1 hadn't been released yet. Everyone knew it was out there, but no one had seen the results yet.

How did you perceive reinforcement learning back then?

No clue. I roughly knew the pipeline but didn't really know the specifics of how industrial-grade language models are trained.

I just went through all the courses I could find, learned everything I could on my own, did all the assignments I could do, and then I hand-rolled a whole system myself. Andrej Karpathy has that famous project called nanoGPT — train a tiny GPT model inside a Google Colab Notebook. I hand-rolled that and then went to the interview.

Two teams reached out — one was doing evaluation, the other was doing reinforcement learning. I chose reinforcement learning. Because it was more unclear.

Anthropic 的初印象与执行力

When I joined, our big team only had about 10 or 11 people. The big team was called Horizon. That big team later basically became the team that covered every aspect of reinforcement learning. There were also teams more focused on data, environments and infrastructure, and teams more focused on research and algorithms.

My impression of Anthropic has actually been pretty consistent — very strong execution. It's a relatively top-down company. After many things are decided, they go all in. The atmosphere between employees is actually very good. Everyone doesn't hide things. Especially when I first joined it was very small.

If you're just doing language model related things — looking back now, that was a very, very good learning opportunity. You could get exposed to every aspect of training this model and find corresponding people to ask.

Coding 押注的起源

Anthropic at that time already had that very firm bet. Where did this bet come from?

One obvious source: after Claude 3 was released, on Twitter many people were discussing that Claude 3 seems to write code better than GPT-4. In that era GPT-4 was still a model with a huge gap from everyone else. So being able to do one important thing better than GPT-4 was quite impressive.

I think at least that's one of the reasons. It was very quick feedback on the market. This is something this company is very strong at — its execution is very, very strong. Once it gets a signal that makes it feel very reasonable, this company will go all in. It doesn't have that redundancy of large organizations.

Why was its coding definitely better than GPT-4? Can't say. There is a reason. But it's a random reason. Not because I chose this so this result happened. It's a purely technical reason. But I can't determine whether it was randomly tried at first or deliberately chosen. If you ask me to guess, I would definitely think it was randomly tried.

At first it might have been bottom-up. But later it became a top-down thing to quickly capture some market.

Anthropic 的独特组织优势

Anthropic, as a company, being able to implement this relatively top-down mechanism is a very unique thing. Why?

Implementing top-down actually has one very difficult point: the person making technical decisions must also be the company's decision maker. First, you have to be technically convincing — then the researchers below will be convinced. On the other hand, you have to be the decision-maker at the company — able to take responsibility.

Anthropic has that going for it. Its technical leader is actually a cofounder of the company — Jared Kaplan and Sam McCandlish. Both are cofounders. They make this decision themselves. It's their company. So they have the authority to do this top-down.

For my work at that time, the person I worked with the most was Jared.

But is this hard for other model companies? Very hard. For example, OpenAI couldn't do it.

When Ilya was there, it might have been possible. But Ilya later, on one hand — I don't know for what reason — he seemed to have lost the ability to make decisions, and then he left.

大公司 vs 初创：不同的剧本

Even Gemini finds it pretty difficult. But Gemini has a completely different playbook.

Big companies and startups — their playbooks are fundamentally different. For startups, what's important is to make bets. If I want to bet, it means there's risk. So I can make decisions very quickly and push decisions through strongly. In this situation, top-down is a big advantage.

But as a big company, it might have a different mindset: not only can I minimize the gambling aspect, but I can also have reserves in every area. If anything succeeds, I can catch up. If I succeed at something myself, I might even take the lead.

Google is a very traditional, very bottom-up organization. At the company level there may be well-defined frameworks to evaluate whether your work is good or bad, to guide you to do things the company needs. But essentially it's still you deciding what you do yourself.

You need at least one leader to have credibility — not the CEO but the #1 technical person. Anthropic's cofounding team — not a single person has left the company. They're a group of people who have truly fought battles together. Many of them were even co-authors on a series of key papers — like the Scaling Law paper (Jared Kaplan, Sam, Dario), the GPT-3 paper (Tom Brown, Benjamin Mann, Jared Kaplan, Sam, Dario).

Claude 3.7：后训练的分水岭

The goal at that time was to do large-scale reinforcement learning and use it to improve coding ability. The final result was everyone trained this 3.7 together — the Claude 3.7 model.

Anthropic at that time probably really had no product ideas — actually calling two models by one name. Later outsiders distinguished by calling the later version of 3.5 as 3.6. So Anthropic followed this outside convention and called the newer model 3.7.

For Anthropic's post-training, 3.7 was a watershed. Before 3.7, post-training was in a relatively small-scale state — more like patching things up.

For a long time, no one really figured out how post-training should scale up. During that period — whether OpenAI or Anthropic or even DeepSeek — they realized how to scale this up. You have to find the right environment where the feedback signal is clear enough and the environment itself is a strong data source. On top of that you can make the training very stable.

Back then no one knew what OpenAI's secret project was — just knew it was called Strawberry. People thought it would bring a new paradigm of post-training reinforcement learning.

When I joined Anthropic, people already had a pretty good idea about the general direction. Later, as I learned more, I discovered that at that moment OpenAI and Anthropic were actually quite different in specific algorithms and ways they used data. In the big picture they're the same — found some regression-like very clear signals, very objective, and the data itself is relatively clean. But the specific implementations differ quite a lot. Each company went in different directions but they all succeeded.

两种范式都还没到天花板

Around the era of 3.7, I once also had the feeling that pre-training was almost "party is over." But later as my understanding deepened, I felt — actually there's still room to do things. Pre-training Scaling Law doesn't tell you to keep getting bigger. It's actually a very systematic framework that can tell you what kinds of things are more effective.

The fact is, later Anthropic and Gemini's pre-training have also been continuously progressing. OpenAI itself was stuck for a long time.

Reaching a plateau has two possibilities. One: the technology itself has reached the limit — you still have things you want the model to do but these technologies just can't teach it. Another: the things you want to do have reached a plateau.

I think now it's the latter. Right now we know — oh, there's a Chatbot, you can teach it to do this. And then there's coding, you can teach it to do this. And then we don't know what else to teach it. The model is still a very smart kid. You can actually teach it many things. But we humans as teachers now don't know what the next thing to teach is.

3.7 的训练过程：4-5 个月

From starting training to release, probably took about four or five months. From when everyone started doing research, probably two or three months. From starting training to completion, with bumps along the way, many things to handle — and there was a lot of new infrastructure. Infrastructure is really important and very time-consuming. Probably about two months.

"我不重要" — 集体主义视角

I don't think I did anything important. My personal contribution — my contribution to any model — my statement is always I feel like I'm not that important to that thing.

More importantly, I was very lucky to have the opportunity to join an important project at that time and did some things. AI in recent years — this thing itself is unstoppable. It doesn't depend on whether you do it or not. If you don't do it, someone else can do it just as well.

So in this era, actually all things that give individuals credit are somewhat suspicious of being hyped. But for me, I am very lucky — being able to join at that stage was a big deal.

Know-how 的陷阱

When it comes to technical tips — this is actually something that on one hand people are really eager to hear about, but companies won't let you talk about, and in reality isn't very useful.

Why? Because a lot of algorithm design isn't actually independent of the algorithm itself. It's very strongly dependent on your infrastructure.

A simple example: during reinforcement learning, the sampler (the one that generates these trace tokens) and the trainer (used to update model weights) — these two machines might be different. The difference is partly due to numerical differences and partly because of using asynchronous training architecture.

Different companies might have different degrees of this difference. So your algorithm design will also differ. Some companies might have these differences being very, very large — then the biggest part of your algorithm might be how to control this and keep training stable. Things like training effectiveness will be weighted slightly less. But some companies might have particularly excellent infrastructure — so they can spend more effort on training effectiveness.

So a lot of these small tips are actually not very useful. Modern AI training is a large system. You need to understand all aspects of this system to have a holistic understanding of what makes something useful because of what — rather than saying the thing itself is useful.

公司规模的文化冲击

Close to 2,000 (people when I left). More than doubled. There were still some rather chaotic phases.

Before, it was just pretty simple — more like a small workshop. Everyone was friends. Everyone knew what the others were doing. No one was doing too much self-promotion or anything like that. No one was doing pointless things. Everyone had a lot on their plate. And the company back then probably had a stronger sense of urgency.

Later on, with more people, this kind of culture would definitely take some hits. There were indeed some people I personally didn't like very much.

I probably don't like people who talk a lot in this field. I think "ideas are cheap". Many ideas are actually quite obvious — everyone knows them. The hard part is how to implement them, how to break it down into small actionable steps and actually get it done. I don't think I like those who spend a large part of their day on Slack talking about grand principles.

离开 Anthropic 的原因

I'd been thinking about it for a month or two.

One aspect: I actually didn't really agree with Dario's anti-China stance. As a company CEO, for him personally, whatever views he holds is fine. But as a CEO, pushing this view to such an extreme was a very emotional reaction. This was a relatively minor reason.

On the bigger picture: there were some cultural shocks at the company. I probably wanted to learn some different things. Anthropic is very focused. You might be doing tool use, Agentic stuff, coding — Anthropic is great. But there are many things Anthropic doesn't do. No one at Anthropic is doing multimodal generation. Anthropic probably didn't spend too much energy on low-level engineering infrastructure.

The anti-China stance — Combined, say 40%. Not the main reason but a very big reason. Not a controlling reason, but a majority holder reason.

当时对 Anthropic 的悲观判断

I think when I left, I was actually quite pessimistic about this company. But later, obviously, I was overly pessimistic.

The reason I was pessimistic: when I left, Anthropic's main revenue source was API — selling tokens. This is a bad business. Because this business is only a good business for one company — Google. Because this business eventually leads to price wars. In price wars, if you don't have the complete chain, there's not much advantage.

But later, Anthropic on the product side — I think there were many clever ideas. Claude Code getting better and better, Claude Cowork, work and efficiency related things — all slowly converged.

If you ask me which of OpenAI and Anthropic would die first — of course they won't really die — just which would become less important first. At that time I would think maybe Anthropic. But later, first OpenAI got punched by Google, then Anthropic itself got on track. Now Anthropic has more advantage.

Anthropic 的产品天赋：Boris Cherny

Claude Code — the person — might really have some opportunities for individual heroism. Boris Cherny. Claude Code almost — at least the beginning of this thing — was him wanting to do this thing himself to improve his own or colleagues' work efficiency. Finally became something important to everyone.

I didn't have too much personal contact with him. He's mainly on the product side. So Anthropic does have a dedicated product department.

This is why product managers might still be quite hard to replace with AI currently. He's not the kind who arranges features. He seems to know how to collaborate with AI — some kind of product manager. The previous generation of product managers might also have some interaction-level changes. But every interaction-level change actually brings a very big product. Maybe Douyin is a product with interaction-level change. Maybe Claude Code is also a product at this level.

加入 Google DeepMind：研究自由

Many researchers, once they enter Google, feel Google doesn't give enough scope. Your move seems to be the opposite.

It depends on what you yourself want. If what you really want is a very clear scope, and this thing is closely related to your final product model — I must get one of my ideas into this model — then Google might be a very bad place. There are so many researchers, so many already mature organizations, doing this thing has a very complicated process.

But Gemini is — if what you want is research freedom, freedom to explore, and want to learn from broader humanity, I think in this world you probably can't find a second place stronger than Gemini.

Many people, when they leave one place for another, the main reason they feel unhappy is because they didn't figure out what they wanted.

不再追求 product impact？

How do you quantify product influence?

Hard to quantify. When publishing papers there was still first author, lead author. Now actually there's no way to quantify. This is also why I think in this era, actually talking about each individual's influence is a very ethereal thing. Essentially it's still the organization that did such a thing, or the world needs this.

在 3.7 上的具体工作

Mainly related to agentic coding and the environment around it, and some algorithmic work. On the algorithmic side, mainly about making training more stable.

But I do think there were definitely some algorithmic improvements that didn't achieve particularly ideal results. To be honest, it's definitely better than previous algorithms. But I don't think that was my personal contribution. It was a collective effort.

The era of individual heroism for language models has probably passed. The Transformer moment — at that point when the technology hadn't yet reached the scale-up stage — the person who discovered it might be a hero. After that, it's been more about collectivism — whether this group can work together toward a common goal.

AI 本质上是简单的

The reason I say collectivism: I think AI as a field is fundamentally simple. Except maybe that leap moment where the idea might require some really deep insights, in the process after that, many ideas are actually very trivial. Anyone could think of them. Anyone could do them. It's just that you got lucky and happened to seize the opportunity.

Before large-scale language models emerged, a lot of things were not inevitable. There was a lot of uncertainty. But after entering that stage, especially now — any organization that wants to stop AI progress can't do it. Anthropic is very concerned about AI safety. But does Anthropic have the ability to stop AI development? It doesn't. If you stop developing, others will continue. Your voice will only get smaller.

The world is pushing us forward rather than us pushing the world forward.

AI 安全：Anthropic 的天真假设

Anthropic's very important motivation is so-called AI safety. The motivation when it was founded.

Anthropic's explanation: First, I need to have the most cutting-edge model. Only then do I have a voice to push my AI safety agenda. So actually its thinking all along has been: I want to build the best model in the world. Everyone will have to listen to me to push forward my safety policies.

But from my personal perspective, I think this idea is very naive. It's not going to happen. What's more likely to happen: everyone will have great frontier models and you won't be able to stop anything.

If you really want to avoid AI bringing about some crisis, what would be a more self-enforcing approach? Like nuclear weapons — multi-party control. Many countries with nuclear weapons. They all have the ability to destroy each other. Stability is maintained through this balance of power.

If you want to stop AI from doing bad things, maybe ultimately you'll need a similar mechanism — rather than pinning your hopes on one company setting a law.

AI 为什么"本质上简单"？

You can run experiments. Compared to things fundamentally difficult — like physics — where without experimental data at that energy scale, you simply can't understand the theory at that energy scale. But AI isn't bound by this. It doesn't matter if you don't understand it. It can still move forward.

I can do any experiment I can think of. It's just that possibly I need some time to scale up the compute or get the infrastructure ready. But there's no fundamental difficulty.

This is why I feel AI doesn't give people the sense that it's hitting a wall. First, you can try many things. Second, it's not that everyone has run out of ideas. More often, there are too many ideas — need to try them one by one.

AI 自己做研究：6-12 个月内

Very soon AI might start doing experiments itself. Within 4 months. In the next 6-12 months AI will do experiments itself.

AI improving itself, speeding up its own development process — this is already happening. It's already helping us achieve some things and speed up our experimental pace.

What it currently can't do: from start to finish complete an AI research project. Not only write code, but run experiment, see results, analyze results, know where it did wrong, propose new hypotheses, design new code, run new experiments. This chain is not yet complete. But this chain might be the next thing to gradually become complete.

离开 Anthropic 后对它的判断

When I left, I was actually quite pessimistic. But later I was overly pessimistic.

Anthropic's API selling tokens — this is a bad business. Only a good business for Google. Eventually leads to price wars.

But later Anthropic on the product side did many clever ideas — Claude Code, Claude Cowork, work efficiency stuff. So now it has become more than what I thought.

If you ask me which of OpenAI and Anthropic would die first — at that time I would think maybe Anthropic. But later, first OpenAI got punched by Google, then Anthropic itself got on track. So now Anthropic has more advantage.

Gemini 转折点：Nano Banana + Gemini 3

I had pretty high expectations for Gemini at the time. I joined at the end of September last year, before Gemini 3 was released.

I think people's perception probably shifted with the Gemini 2.5 generation. 2.5 was a generation where people actually started using the model.

Why did you join Gemini? Mainly because I knew what kind of atmosphere Gemini had — a lot of people doing different kinds of research. And many Gemini engineers — their technical skills are extremely, extremely strong.

In terms of actual impact, two things created a major turning point for Gemini: Nano Banana and Gemini 3. Two things back to back.

If there were only Gemini 3, it probably wouldn't have had such great results. Because when your market share is less than 10%, whether your model is slightly better or worse — it just spreads too slowly. What Nano Banana did: first, it went viral in the market. Then a ton of people downloaded the Gemini app. And then Gemini 3 was released right after — retaining those users.

If Gemini hadn't thrown this punch, OpenAI's position would be really comfortable. When ordinary people use models, their perception of the model's capabilities is actually very, very weak. Most people don't even use the o-series models. Most people just use the regular ChatGPT.

OpenAI 救了 Google 一命

As an outsider, in a sense I think OpenAI saved Google's life. Because everyone used to worry: this chatbot would completely replace search. If this really happened, Google would actually be in a tough spot. But fortunately OpenAI did this thing first, then made Google realize this thing is important. But it didn't take this thing all the way — didn't completely kill off search. Maybe just ate some market share. As a result, let Google itself catch up on chatbots.

If a company in a fictional world not only made a chatbot but also marched forward triumphantly, doing better and better, really just ate up your search in one go — completely didn't give you a chance to fight back — then it would be very tough.

Why didn't chatbots eat up search? Because current chatbot interaction methods actually won't completely eat up search. The one point it's stronger than search: strong interactivity, follow-up questions, condensing complex information. So this portion of usage scenarios will steal people from search.

But there are still some very simple scenarios in search. Like I just search "buy rice" — search and it's done. Do I have to ask ChatGPT which one is good? It's still spinning there.

Google 的产品困境

Google has traditionally been a bit slow with products. Has always been relatively slow.

What's Google good at? Finding an extremely simple product form. Everyone looks the same. Then it just competes with you relentlessly on technology. And you can't outcompete it. Search is a classic example. Everyone has the same search box. One button — but it just searches faster and more accurately than you. There's nothing you can do about it.

All along, Google has been in this state of doing very well but Wall Street never really bought into it. Everyone always wondered where this company's moat really is. There's no product ingenuity. No retention mechanisms either. But it has survived until now.

It's the culture — particularly valued engineers in the past, later particularly valued research. Very well suited for products where technological capability spills over.

"聊天框"是终极形态吗？

I don't think anyone's position is secure right now. The form of AI still has a long way to go. We're not at any endgame yet.

Conditioned on the chatbot being the super app, then maybe there's something to fight over. But the problem is: is this form the super app form? What if someone comes out with a completely different form one day, and your functionality becomes a subset of that thing?

This model clearly has so many capabilities. But the way we use it is a chatbot. It just doesn't quite make sense.

(Note: This video was recorded over 2 months ago, when the agent paradigm was not yet clear.)

We need a product manager to unlock the model's capabilities.

Google 内部的变化

Google's technical reserves have always been sufficient. Organizationally, it became increasingly clear later on — having a better framework to let everyone work together. There might slowly be some progress.

Especially pre-training has become very, very clear now. Who is responsible for what, every node, who is the responsible person. These things are very clear. It was very chaotic in the earliest days.

Pre-training at Google is a very, very controllable thing — predictable. You can know the next generation won't be bad. You might even know how good it will be.

Compared to Anthropic, it's still more bottom-up. Different cultures can both work. Big companies have big company ways. Startups have startup ways.

Google more says — for relatively deterministic things like pre-training, which is already a relatively deterministic paradigm — Google will be more like making it into an engineering project. Google's engineering management ability is very strong.

Post-training of course has more uncertainty. So maybe post-training currently is still more bottom-up — everyone can try more broadly.

Pre-training 也是一种 RL？

It's hard to say from a pure technical perspective. Pre-training, or supervised learning — what is the essential difference between SFT and RL?

Pre-training and SFT are essentially not that different. You just take the data you get as your ground truth, treat that as your expert output, then you align toward the distribution of that expert output.

Reinforcement learning might be a broader level: this original output is not a given expert but something I produced myself. Among them there are good results and bad results. So you want good results to move closer and bad results to move away.

In a sense, pre-training and SFT are a subset of reinforcement learning. But these two things do have differences. The biggest difference lies in the data.

For pre-training data, what matters more is having a good distribution — broad enough, aligned well enough with the scope you want. But data quality doesn't need to be extremely high.

For post-training, it's the opposite. Distribution may be much narrower. But quality requirements are very high.

不同公司如何组织 pre/post-training？

Anthropic and Google are pretty similar — both have one team for pre-training and another for post-training.

OpenAI might be more chaotic. Initially they had three teams — pre-training, the Strawberry team (reinforcement learning), and a post-training team. My understanding: its post-training wasn't really its RL team. Strawberry and its post-training are actually what other companies call post-training and product. They treat the later stages as product work — their post-training is intertwined with product.

Most companies, the product team doesn't really train models anymore. They communicate the desired traits to the team training the model. But OpenAI's post-training in a sense is its own product team but it can also train models.

近期模型发布的洞察

I think I can only say I was fortunate to be involved.

Every time I see it I feel like — how am I going to face my colleagues in the office tomorrow. At the office it's fine. Probably they don't care too much.

Every project I've been part of, whether at Google or Anthropic, would happen even without me. Everyone now is a surfer. Essentially it's a wave — not you the surfer. The wave is AI. This thing itself will move forward. Whether you surf this wave or not, this wave will crash on shore.

Benchmark 饱和与新方向

Some things people are competing over — actually looking at it now, in this era, already not that important. Because of inertia from the past, everyone would compete for first place on various Benchmarks to prove basic capability is strong. But by now, those Benchmarks are somewhat maxed out.

Earliest everyone paid attention to SWE-bench — randomly everyone hit 80-something. Fortunately no one exceeded 83 (recently OpenAI said they exceeded 83). Some problems are not well-defined.

Before everyone reasoned with AIME, then IMO. After IMO — RKGI. Before Gemini 3, everyone probably forgot — the highest was maybe level 10 — everyone was like "hard as climbing to heaven." Then Gemini 3 made it 30-something. Then Claude 4.6 became 60-something. Then Gemini 3 Deep Think hit 80-something. So this is also maxed out.

Releasing fast also shows this problem has become easy for everyone. Everyone knows the know-how now. There are no secrets anymore.

下一个范式级变化？

The two things I just mentioned — ML coding and long horizon — I think might be something that hasn't reached paradigm-level change but is very valuable.

ML coding is valuable because Google itself is a major player in AI research and the most full-stack — has hardware design, parts connecting hardware to models. If this entire system can be accelerated or better managed, that could be very valuable.

Long horizon goes without saying. Everyone knows it's very important.

Paradigm-level might still be those more uncertain things like multimodal generation. There might be a hero or group of heroes.

Continue learning 与 World models

People used to think continue learning and long horizon were very different. Continue learning changes some of the model's weights. When you do this context management, it doesn't change model weights. But actually, if you think about it, there's no fundamental difference. Because those tokens in the context — their own KV cache is also a kind of weight.

Which one will be more useful? It's unclear. But essentially they're both for doing long horizon.

Ten thousand people have ten thousand world models. The definition isn't clear. First of all, I don't know what a world model is. When everyone talks about world models, they might be talking about different things. Gemini's world model might be different from labs like Fei-Fei Li's.

Gemini's world model is more of an end-to-end level of training. Result: not only can I generate a video, I can generate a scenario — the state at this moment, and given an action condition, the next moment state becomes a function of previous state and action. End-to-end training this capability.

xAI：commentator 视角

xAI I don't understand. They've always been quite turbulent.

I don't have that much contact with xAI. Some people I contacted have also left now. Actually I don't know what happened to them.

Google 内部的英雄

I think heroes might be different people at different stages. But behind every hero there is one person — Sergey Brin, Google's cofounder. Many big decisions might not be decided by him on how to do them, but in the end he has to be the one to make the final call.

The person who appears more on the front lines is Koray Kavukcuoglu (DeepMind CTO, now also Google SVP).

Demis Hassabis — I think Demis might manage more things leaning toward science, like drug design, Isomorphic Labs. Gemini — he doesn't manage much. At least from my perspective, the person I see more is Koray.

系统性做 AI 的方法

Several aspects:

One: from the whole system perspective, it needs a relatively scientific attitude. You need to clearly understand Scaling Law. You need to clearly understand what assumptions you have made. When I make a change, what factors are actually related and what factors are not.

From the organizational perspective: requires people to be very reliable, very responsible. Every system, every evaluation framework is very easily hacked. You can always do something to make your metrics look good. But a trustworthy person would think: if the thing I did works well, is it really effective at large scales? Did I miss some factors?

Actually doing things systematically — sounds like one sentence but actually very complex. Many details, many resistances. It actually goes against human nature. Every individual's human nature might be to make their own things show up better. But for a company, the most beneficial thing is to make the entire system very solid. This is actually the best for you personally. Because once this system is solid, you can leverage this system to produce more output.

The bad thing: this system will make your individual heroism not shine. But you can rest assured that others' individual heroism also won't shine. If you are in a system where individual heroism can shine, then this system might not be particularly stable. Because one person leaving might cause the entire thing to collapse. For example like OpenAI.

重复做简单事的难度

The most difficult thing in this industry is actually doing simple things cleanly. Why? Because everyone can do simple things. If you can't do them cleaner than others, then there's no value.

需要研究员自己对于这个系统怎么运作有一个好的理解，然后以及对公司负责任才能做到。否则就是你很容易做到一件事——你可能比如说你在考虑training的时候是比别人好的，但你考虑training加sampling时候比别人差。你总可以选择你只是有training，但这就很糟糕。

所以这个就是既需要你个人的负责任，又需要说组织所建立的这个体系里能够能尽量的发现这些有意的或者无意的这种边界的事情。

但是你作为个体的话，你不知道怎么样是对全局最好的呀？其实是需要——我觉得如果一个研究员做不到对全局去考虑的话，他就不是一个好的研究员，在现在这个时代。

这和你就是在学术界做research是很不一样的事。因为在学术界做research，本质上是一个人吃饱全家不愁的状态——我为我的项目负责，我为我的可重复性负责。但是在一个公司里，你其实更多的时候是——我得为这个公司负责。这是两种完全不一样的心态。

那你这种自觉性从哪里来的？不知道。我觉得我可能就是拉不下脸。

拉不下脸是什么？就是——你对一个公司负责任是你和这个公司的契约的一部分。其实我觉得没什么道理不这么做。这么做是没有原因的。

组织：top-down vs bottom-up

If you're just doing it for personal heroism, it's very likely to undermine the bigger picture.

The traditional view: bottom-up was a necessary condition for fostering innovation. Because everyone needs freedom — only with freedom can there be innovation. But purely bottom-up — you find it doesn't actually work either. Because it just becomes chaotic. That's what Google was like before.

You probably need someone or a small group who can blend these two approaches. That's why I think whether an organization runs well or not — it looks like an organizational issue but ultimately comes down to the tech leader.

Two qualities for a leader: 1. Can fight fires themselves — when something runs into trouble, can step in and lead the team to solve the problem. 2. Can understand others — even if it's something they wouldn't do themselves, they can understand why what others are doing matters. They can tolerate and accommodate others.

TPU vs GPU

From a purely hardware perspective, it's hard to say which is truly better.

The biggest difference setting aside hardware: GPUs have a better open-source ecosystem. TPUs don't. But this actually isn't an issue at large-scale commercial deployment. If you're running a cluster of hundreds of thousands of cards, building out infrastructure isn't really that big of a deal.

These two have differences in design philosophy. Hopper GPU: inside one pod, maybe just eight cards, all interconnect with one another. NVLink is extremely fast. Within one pod, basically no communication bandwidth bottleneck.

TPUs: abandoned pairwise interconnection between cards. They try to fit as many cards as possible into one big rack. 3D Torus design — each card only connects to its three nearest neighbors in three directions, but the entire cluster can be connected into one big Torus. If your compilers or sharding logic is written well enough, you get more memory capacity and reduce a lot of communication bounds.

Downside: at small scale, more of a rigid structure. General versatility might not be as strong.

Neo Labs：大部分会死

My feeling is that the vast majority of neo labs will die. Some labs genuinely have good people. Some labs might actually be starting to do some real work — for example, Thinking Machines is delivering some new things. But some neo labs — I have absolutely no idea what they're trying to do. These two have actually been away from the field for a long time.

中国消费应用 vs 美国生产力软件

In 2026, China will place a lot of emphasis on the consumer-side narrative. Who becomes that super app.

Nobody in Silicon Valley talks about this. Because American enterprise — the productivity software market is just too big and profit margins are too high. So now everyone will probably focus first on productivity software or enterprise.

What China is really strong at is the consumer side. It can come up with really complex product features in a very indirect way to snowball that profit.

Douyin — not "watch a video, charge 20 cents per video." You can watch videos for free, but I can quietly slip in ads, do live streaming, do e-commerce.

But that doesn't work for productivity software. Productivity software is very straightforward: I help you write code, my cost is 150 a month, I sell it to you for 200, I make 50.

The US in the past — with very straightforward products it can push technology to the extreme. But there's never been a product that felt so sophisticated that you can't live without it, yet you don't feel like it's taking your money, but it's actually making money from you.

Should Meta copy ByteDance? Meta isn't as strong as ByteDance. Meta can't find its own niche. No American company doing this. No one has found the niche that Doubao occupies.

The Americans making products — the people doing consumer products aren't good enough. Far behind China.

The positive feedback loop in the US over the past decade all came from B2B. A lot of enterprise stuff. Or it's just too easy to make money in the US. When it's too easy to make money, you won't rack your brains over how to make money.

招聘哲学：可靠 > 聪明

I'd benefit from this. But sometimes — I don't know if this thing is a good thing.

The price has become so high. On one hand, everyone thinks this thing is scarce. But it might not be that scarce. Because training a person — although this thing isn't that hard — but training a person requires an environment. You need to have that opportunity to be exposed to this thing. Without that opportunity, no matter how smart you are, it's useless. Maybe in the past, people who could encounter that opportunity weren't that many.

Another aspect: maybe the hype about people is excessive. Really like to mythologize individuals.

The most important trait is being reliable.

我设计的面试题

This interview question: this person needs to, within 24 hours, complete a reinforcement learning project from scratch. They choose what model to use, what data, what algorithm. After 24 hours up, they'll have a one-hour discussion with me.

Without AI, this would be impossible. No one could do it in 24 hours. With AI, it's actually quite easy. AI can do the whole thing for you.

Two reasons for this design:

  1. In this era, evaluating whether they write good code is useless. What's more important: whether they can effectively leverage AI.
  2. There's a trap: if you let AI do everything but don't really try to understand what AI did for you, you'll be exposed during that one-hour discussion. That's where people fail. Tests whether you've truly formed a collaboration with AI.

Also, why 24 hours: to see how much this person values this opportunity. Can they stay up all night? If willing to pull an all-nighter, they can survive these 24 hours.

AI 是否还是蓝海？

Purely working on language models is no longer a blue ocean. The last train has already left. I feel like I got in on that last train. New people won't have the opportunity to encounter such good opportunities in a relatively small team.

But AI is a very vast field. Language models are just a tiny part. Multimodal generation — many opportunities. Robotics — even more opportunities. Even more extreme: using AI to help with real scientific problems, quantum control. More blue ocean.

For people young enough, doing the hottest thing right now is not the right choice. Doing things no one has done now might be a better choice.

未来规划

I think probably not (be at Google for a long time).

I will still try to challenge myself. Need to torture myself. But I just might need to find something worth torturing myself for.
